# Search, SEO, and Content Farms

> **Use boundary:** This file is written for research, ethics, detection, and policy analysis. It describes mechanisms at a conceptual level so they can be studied, criticised, or detected. It is not an execution guide for manipulating platforms, audiences, investors, advertisers, or creators.


## Domain summary

Manufactured traction appears as scaled content abuse, site reputation abuse, expired domain abuse, low-value programmatic SEO, AI content farms, and affiliate-content laundering.

## Signals commonly targeted

- search rank
- domain authority
- topical coverage
- content freshness
- backlink reputation

## Why this domain matters

This domain matters because its proxy signals are routinely treated as evidence of quality, popularity, safety, usefulness, or legitimacy. When those proxy signals are corrupted, downstream systems allocate attention and money incorrectly.

## Typical ethical boundary

The boundary is crossed when activity hides material context, makes paid activity look independent, fabricates engagement, manipulates ranking signals, or encourages audiences to infer real demand from distorted evidence.

## Examples to study

- Google March 2024 spam-policy update
- 45% less low-quality unoriginal content after rollout

## Source notes

- **Google Search March 2024 update / spam policies** (Platform policy / primary). Targets scaled content abuse, site reputation abuse, and expired domain abuse. URL: https://developers.google.com/search/blog/2024/03/core-update-spam-policies
- **Google Search update results — 45% less low-quality unoriginal content** (Platform blog / primary). Google said its March 2024 rollout produced 45% less low-quality, unoriginal content in Search results. URL: https://blog.google/products/search/google-search-update-march-2024/
